coredata.dc:description: The design of therapeutic nanoplatforms based on fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has become a viable strategy because of their aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, and ease of further functionalization. By doping various heteroatoms into pristine CDs structures, we synthesized N-, Cl-, and S-doped CDs (NClS/CDs), as well as Se-, N-, and Cl-doped CDs (NClSe/CDs) with superior optoelectronic properties using rapid and straightforward microwave heating. The quantum efficiencies of these NClS/CDs and NClSe/CDs were enhanced to 30.7 % and 42.9 %, respectively, compared to those of undoped CDs (0.66 %). Owing to their better light absorption properties, NClS/CDs efficiently produced reactive oxygen species (ROS) under 532 nm laser irradiation for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Considering the ROS generation and surface carrier abilities of NClS/CDs, we designed the loading of camptothecin (CPT) drug via a thioketal linker (TL), resulting in h/CDs@CPT nanovesicles (NVs) with a drug-loading efficiency of 46.5 %. Under laser irradiation in an acidic environment, ROS-triggered CPT release was observed, with 50.2 % of CPT released following the breakdown of the ROS-sensitive TL. In vitro cellular studies revealed that h/CDs@CPT NVs possessed minimal cytotoxicity toward HeLa and 4 T1 cancer cells, despite the high clinical efficacy of PDT and ROS-induced chemotherapeutic response under laser treatment. Confocal microscopy of HeLa and 4 T1 cells revealed that h/CDs@CPT NVs produced red-emissive photographs for potential cancer cell detection. Therefore, our study presents an image-guided PDT and chemotherapeutic platform based on h/CDs@CPT NVs, which will be an attractive candidate for future cancer treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of NCl/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs. (a) UV–visible absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of NCl/CDs treated with various concentration of PA. (c) The UV–visible absorption and (d) fluorescence spectra of NClS/CDs treated with varying amount of LC. (e) UV–visible absorption and (f) fluorescence spectra of NClSe/CDs treated with varying ratio of LS. The absorbance spectra were measured from 200 nm to 700 nm wavelength region, and fluorescence spectra were obtained using 580 nm excitation wavelength.

XPS studies of NClS/CDs. (a) Survey XPS spectra for N/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs measured from 0 eV to 600 eV binding energy. HR-XPS analysis of NClS/CDs for (b) S 2p; (c) Cl 2p; (d) C 1 s; (e) N 1 s; and (f) O 1 s atoms.

HR-XPS analysis for NClSe/CDs. Deconvoluted spectra of (a) C 1 s; (b) N 1 s; (c) O 1 s; (d) Cl 2p; and (e) Se 3d orbitals. (f) Time resolved PL lifetime of N/CDs, NCl/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs solutions.
Material characterizations of h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs. (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of h/CDs. (c) TEM image and (d) DLS analysis for h/CDs@CPT NVs.

Photodynamic and chemotherapeutic studies of h/CDs@CPT NVs. (a) UV absorption spectral measurements for the degradations of DPBF solution in the presence of h/CDs@CPT NVs. (b) ESR spectra of TEMP solution upon combinations with h/CDs@CPT at various concentrations and laser irradiation (532 nm laser source; 1.12 W/cm2). (c) Cumulative CPT releasing phenomena performed using different variables such as dark condition, H2O2 solution, laser irradiation, Fe2+/H2O2, and laser/Fe2+/H2O2. The experiment was conducted for 48 h reaction time. The error bars were obtained from thrice experiment. (d) Cellular uptaking of h/CDs@CPT NVs via endocytosis pathways and ROS-cleavable drug releasing mechanism upon laser irradiation.

In vitro biocompatibility and fluorescent imaging of h/CDs@CPT NVs. Biocompatibility studies for (a) HeLa and (b) 4 T1 cancer cells incubated with different concentrations of h/CDs solution for 24 and 48 h. Statistical significance T-test: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001. In vitro cytotoxicity evaluation of h/CDs@CPT NVs against (c) HeLa and (d) 4 T1 cells through PDT and chemotherapy mechanism. 532 nm laser source was used to activate ROS generation and CPT release. Fluorescent imaging of (e) HeLa cells using various concentration of h/CDs@CPT NVs and incubated for 8 h. DAPI marker was used as nucleus staining agent. Red channel indicates h/CDs@CPT NVs, and merge pictures are overlay of DAPI and h/CDs@CPT NVs images together. Blue and red cellular images was obtained using 405 and 561 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. The scale bar is 30 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vitro ROS generation efficacy of h/CDs@CPT NVs. (a) Hydrolysis reaction of DCFH-DA to DCFH intermediate through intracellular esterase, and then undergo oxidation with ROS to produce green emitting DCF• molecule. In vitro fluorescence imaging of (b) HeLa cells incubated with different ratio of h/CDs@CPT NVs. DAPI staining agent was employed to fix the nucleus structure. Green images represent DCF• molecule which was formed during the reaction between ROS and DCFH inside cells structure. 532 nm laser power (1.12 W/cm2) was utilized to activate the generations of ROS. Red images indicate for h/CDs@CPT NVs. Blue, green, and red confocal images was obtained using 405, 488, and 561 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. The scale bar is 30 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Schematic illustrations of the room temperature synthesis of h/CDs@CPT NVs using h/CDs and TLCPT through crosslinkers. The preparations of TLCPT prodrug was done in the presence of TCBC and TEA organic compounds. The right-side diagram displays ROS generation properties of h/CDs@CPT NVs upon laser irradiation and intracellular ROS-triggered CPT release phenomena for PDT and chemotherapy of cancer.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of therapeutic nanoplatforms based on fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has become a viable strategy because of their aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, and ease of further functionalization. By doping various heteroatoms into pristine CDs structures, we synthesized N-, Cl-, and S-doped CDs (NClS/CDs), as well as Se-, N-, and Cl-doped CDs (NClSe/CDs) with superior optoelectronic properties using rapid and straightforward microwave heating. The quantum efficiencies of these NClS/CDs and NClSe/CDs were enhanced to 30.7 % and 42.9 %, respectively, compared to those of undoped CDs (0.66 %). Owing to their better light absorption properties, NClS/CDs efficiently produced reactive oxygen species (ROS) under 532 nm laser irradiation for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Considering the ROS generation and surface carrier abilities of NClS/CDs, we designed the loading of camptothecin (CPT) drug via a thioketal linker (TL), resulting in h/CDs@CPT nanovesicles (NVs) with a drug-loading efficiency of 46.5 %. Under laser irradiation in an acidic environment, ROS-triggered CPT release was observed, with 50.2 % of CPT released following the breakdown of the ROS-sensitive TL. In vitro cellular studies revealed that h/CDs@CPT NVs possessed minimal cytotoxicity toward HeLa and 4 T1 cancer cells, despite the high clinical efficacy of PDT and ROS-induced chemotherapeutic response under laser treatment. Confocal microscopy of HeLa and 4 T1 cells revealed that h/CDs@CPT NVs produced red-emissive photographs for potential cancer cell detection. Therefore, our study presents an image-guided PDT and chemotherapeutic platform based on h/CDs@CPT NVs, which will be an attractive candidate for future cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer cells exhibit higher reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels than normal cells, maintain redox homeostasis by triggering antioxidant pathways, and tend to be more susceptible to oxidative stress-induced cell death [1–3]. Thus, developing water-dispersible and biocompatible nanoparticles (NPs) able to generate ROS upon exposure to external stimuli or agents to induce extreme oxidative stress in cancer cells is a promising strategy for selectively destroying tumor cells [4–6]. To date, multiple forms of ROS-based anticancer weapons have been developed. Among these, photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a promising strategy for effective cancer cell elimination because of its low drug resistance, non-invasiveness, high penetration depth, and minimal detrimental effects on normal cells. Upon photoirradiation, a photosensitizer (PS) agent is excited to a higher energy level and transformed to a triplet state through intersystem crossing (ISC), thereby accelerating the generation of ROS via energy and charge transfer pathways [7–10]. The unregulated growth and expansion of tumor blood vessels imposes reduced oxygen availability to tumor areas, resulting in a hypoxic environment and inhibiting ROS-based therapeutic efficacy. Overcoming these hypoxic conditions is necessary for the functioning of efficient PDT agents, and thus critical for effective future nanomedicine [11,12]. In recent years, several PDT agents such as chlorin e6, porphyrin, organic dyes (i.e., lipophilic cationic heptamethine dye (IR780), indocyanine green (ICG), ICG analog (IR820) molecules), and metal quantum dots (QDs) have been utilized [13–17]. Despite the advantages of PDT, the poor water solubility and higher levels of toxicity have hampered their applicability in nanomedicine [18–20]. As such, carbon dots (CDs) may present a viable alternative to current PS agents because of their outstanding water dispersibility and biocompatibility. CDs are fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials with outstanding optoelectronic features, including tunable photoluminescence (PL), high color purity, excellent biocompatibility, and remarkable stability when exposed to light and thermal stimuli [21–26]. The PL properties of CDs have garnered significant interest recently, with the intent to boost PL quantum efficiencies and alter their optical and electrical properties by choosing an appropriate carbon source, altering the reaction conditions, and employing post-treatment strategies such as surface passivation and purification technologies [27–31]. Despite their outstanding optical properties, CDs possess a lower quantum yield (QYs) compared with other families of QDs owing to the high content of defect structures, surface trap states, and poor purification technology. It has been suggested that heteroatom doping may improve the optical properties of CDs by increasing the radiative recombination rate, as well as removing surface trap states and defect structures [23,30,32,33]. More recently, several studies have been conducted to enhance the CDs quantum efficiencies. For example, a novel approach was reported where heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), chlorine (Cl), sulfur (S), fluorine (F), and phosphorous (P) atoms were introduced into CDs to improve their optical properties and QYs [34–38]. Jin et al. revealed that phosphorus-doped CDs (P/CDs) can exhibit higher QYs of > 20 % compared with unpassivated CDs. Additionally, compared with C atoms, introducing a less electronegative P atom may shift the Fermi level toward the conduction band as it is an electron donor, thereby altering the electrical properties. Compared to single heteroatom-doped CDs, multi-atom-doped CDs exhibited a higher fluorescence QYs and longer wave-emission maximum [39–41]. Cui et al. used a one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize dual-functional NS/CDs using acrylic acid and methionine. The obtained NS/CDs exhibited colorful luminescence, good optical stability, a high QYs of 10.55 %, and a fluorescence lifetime of 7.92 ns [42]. Wang et al. fabricated water-soluble NSP/CDs via one-step hydrothermal treatment using heteroatom-enriched naturally extracted cucumber juice. Although the highest QYs was only 3.25 %, the obtained NSP/CDs exhibited multicolor luminescence, a good luminescence stability, high resistance to photobleaching, and excitation-dependent emission behavior [43].

Recent advances in nanotechnology have enabled the simultaneous integration of PS agents and drug molecules into a single platform, improving anticancer efficacy via multiple mechanistic pathways and enhancing the aqueous solubility of hydrophobic drugs, reducing clearance by the reticuloendothelial system, and reducing non-specific drug toxicity. There have been reports of ROS-sensitive prodrug delivery systems; for example, the thioketal (TK) linkage is an essential ROS-cleavable linkage; however, the endogenous ROS level in the body is inadequate to rupture the TK linkage [44–46]. Therefore, via combination with a PS agent, the concentration of ROS may be elevated to disrupt the TK linkage effectively, resulting in drug molecule release and severely damaging the cancer cells. Using this feature, the ROS-sensitive prodrug delivery system enabled the regulation of intratumoral drug release under laser treatment. PS-loaded prodrugs, such as chlorin e6-loaded TK-linked doxorubicin and phthalocyanine-loaded TK-linked camptothecin (CPT) hybrid NPs, have recently been produced [47]. However, weak PS encapsulation in the nanocarrier system causes leakage during blood circulation, which may cause massive damage to normal tissues and reduce the ROS generation efficiency [44,48,49]. Thus, owing to their ROS production capability, ease of functionalization, water solubility, and biocompatibility, heteroatom-doped CDs can overcome the existing challenges in constructing prodrug-tethered therapeutic agents to reach tumor zones concurrently and synchronously.

In this study, a one-pot microwave technique was established to produce highly luminescent CDs using p-phenyldiamine as a carbon source and various heteroatoms such as selenium (Se), chlorine (Cl), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N). The resulting CDs (NCl/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs) exhibited enhanced photoluminescence intensities compared to undoped N/CDs. Surface trap states and defect structures were eliminated by adding N, Cl, S, and Se to the CDs, and the PL lifetime was improved, yielding enhanced quantum efficiencies of both NClS/CDs and NClSe/CDs. Interestingly, NClS/CDs (h/CDs) demonstrated ROS-generating properties under 532 nm laser irradiation used for PDT. To maximize therapeutic efficacy, CPT was conjugated with h/CDs via a thioketal linker (TL), yielding h/CDs@CPT nanovesicles (NVs). The drug-releasing properties of the h/CDs/CPT NVs were evaluated in the presence of ROS generation under acidic conditions. In vitro studies confirmed that the h/CDs@CPT NVs were readily ingested by the cancer cells using an endocytosis mechanism and exhibited better biocompatibility and induced cancer cell death in the presence of laser illumination and an acidic tumor environment. Overall, the h/CDs/CPT NVs exhibited high therapeutic efficacy toward cancer cells via chemotherapy and PDT platforms.

P-phenyldiamine (p-PD, >99 %), l-cysteine (LC, ∼98 %), l-selenocysteine (LS, ∼98 %), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, 98 %) anhydrous acetone (99.8 %), 2,4,6-trichlorobenzoylchloride (TCBC, 98 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.8 %), 1,3-diphenylisobezofuran (DPBF, 99 %) and perchloric acid (PA, 70 %) were obtained from Acros organics. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.8 %) was obtained from J.T. Baker. Dichloromethane (DCM, 99.5 %) and ethanol (99.55) were bought from ECHO Chemical Co. ltd. 3‑Mercaptopropionoic acid (MPA, 99 %), 4-Dimethylaminopyridine (99 %), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC, 97 %), and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Triethylamine (TEA, 99 %), rhodamine B (RhB, 95 %), sodium pyruvate (100 mM), sodium bicarbonate (99.5–100 %), non-essential amino acid solution (100x), antibiotic antimycotic solution (100x), l-glutamine (20 mM), trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25 %), Eagle's Minimal Essential Medium (MEME), Dulbecco’s modified eagle’s medium high glucose (DMEM), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) medium, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, ≥99 %), 2,7-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, ≥97 %), 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, 98 %), and tryphan blue solution (0.4 w/w%) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was collected from Thermo Scientific HyClone. Hydrogen peroxide (30 %) was obtained from Honeywell Fluka™. WST-1 (4-[3-(4-Iodophenyl)-2-[4-nitrophenyl]–2H-5-tetrazolio]-1,3‑benzene disulfonate) ordered from Mannheim Co. ltd. The reagents were used without purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of N/CDs, NCl/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Heteroatom-doped CDs were produced using microwave pyrolysis. In brief, p-phenyldiamine (p-PD) was used as the principal carbon source, whereas l-cysteine (LC), l-selenocysteine (LS), and perchloric acid (PA) functioned as sources of nitrogen (N)/sulfur (S), nitrogen (N)/selenium (Se), and chlorine (Cl) dopants, respectively. To produce an NClS/CDs solution, 0.55 mmol of p-PD and 0.01 mmol of LC were poured into a microwave tube containing 15 mL of deionized water (DI water). After the mixture was sonicated for a couple of minutes, 0.6 mmol of PA solution was added, and the overall solution was then heated in a microwave reactor to 200 °C for 10 min. The solution (NClS/CDs) was cooled to room temperature before being centrifuged using a 1 k MWCO advanced centrifuge tube at 4000 rpm for 20 min. After completion of the reaction, the red-emitting NClS/CDs solution was filtered using a filter paper with pore size 0.22 µM and stored in a refrigerator. In another experiment, NClSe/CDs were synthesized by substituting LC with 0.02 mmol of LS, with no other changes in procedure. For NCl/CDs, 0.6 mmol of PA and 0.55 mmol of p-PD were thoroughly dispersed in 15 mL of DI water. On the other hand, pristine CDs (N/CDs) were produced by dissolving 0.55 mmol of p-PD in 15 mL of DI water (without any dopant ions). All other reaction parameters and purification techniques remained as described for the preparation of NClS/CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrications of ROS-sensitive TL and TLCPT prodrug molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Biosynthesis of the TL has been accomplished in recent studies [44]. Briefly, 39.68 mmol of MPA (39.68 mol), 19.48 mmol of anhydrous acetone, and 11.68 mmol of TFA were poured into a sample vial and gently stirred at room temperature for 40 min until crystallization was complete. After washing with hexane and cold water, the resulting TL was filtered through a 0.45 m, freeze-dried, and stored in a refrigerator.

Following this, Yamaguchi esterification was utilized to develop a TL conjugated camptothecin (TLCPT) prodrug molecule. Specifically, 1 mmol of TL powder was dissolved in 10 mL of DMF, followed by the addition of 3 mmol TEA, 1 mmol TCBC, and 0.2 mmol of DMAP. The mixture was then mixed at room temperature for 10 min. Then, 0.5 mmol of the CPT solution (174.1 mg CPT in 10 mL DMF) was injected into the mixture and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The reaction was neutralized with DI water before isolation using DCM and saturated brine water. Finally, excess water was removed through suction filtration with sodium sulfate, and the TLCPT was concentrated to form a solid. The crude product was stored in a refrigerator.

The prodrug molecule was loaded onto the h/CDs surface using combined organic crosslinkers. To activate the carboxyl group of the TLCPT molecule, 17.2 µmol of EDC and 17.2 µmol of NHS-sulfo were added to 2.5 mL of TLCPT solution (4 mg/mL in DMSO) and rapidly stirred for 2 h. Then, 40 mg of h/CDs was added and stirred overnight at room temperature. Post-reaction, the mixture was washed with DI water and centrifuged several times to remove unreacted substance and DMSO residue. Finally, a solid product (denoted as h/CDs@CPT NVs) was obtained by freeze-drying.

The potential of h/CDs@CPT NVs to produce radical species was determined using 1,4-diphenyl-2,3-benzofuran (DPBF), an ROS-responsive molecule, and a 532 nm laser irradiation source. A combination of 0.4 mL of 100 ppm-h/CDs@CPT NVs and 0.1 mL of DPBF (1.5 mM) was thoroughly mixed. The mixture was subsequently irradiated with a 532 nm laser (1.12 W/cm2) for 30 min. Quenching of the fluorescent DPBF molecule was validated by obtaining the absorption spectra at 416 nm every 5 min. The obtained results were compared to those of a control experiment that used either DPBF alone or laser illumination.

The ROS generation properties of h/CDs@CPT NVs were confirmed using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. In this experiment, 100 mM of TEMP probe molecule was produced in DMSO and finely mixed with 100 ppm of h/CDs@CPT NVs solution. The admixture was treated for 10 min with a 532 nm laser source (1.12 W/cm2) prior to the EPR measurement. EPR spectra for TEMP were obtained using h/CDs@CPT NVs or a laser source alone. Spectral data were collected and analyzed.

The applicability and efficacy of the h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs in biomedical applications were further validated by in vitro cancer cell investigations. In this experiment, two types of cancer cell lines, human cervical cancer (HeLa) and mammary carcinoma breast (4 T1) cancer cells, were employed to evaluate the biocompatibility and therapeutic efficacy of the h/CDs@CPT NVs. First, 4 T1 cell lines were supplemented with a RPMI-1640 medium composed of 2 mM l-glutamine, 10 mM HEPES, 1 mM sodium pyruvate, 4500 mg/L glucose, 1500 mg/L sodium bicarbonate, and 10 % fetal bovine serum. For the HeLa cell lines, an EMEM medium was utilized, containing 1 % antibiotic–antimycotic component, 1 % nonessential amino acids, 1 % l-glutamine, 1 % sodium pyruvate, and 10 % FBS. The cells were then moved to an incubator fixed at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were successively washed using PBS, and 100 µL of fresh medium was dispersed into the cells. Then, a varied amount of the h/CDs@CPT NVs (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 ppm) was supplied to the cells and incubated for varying intervals (24 h and 48 h). The cells were treated with fresh medium and 10 µL of the WST-1 reagent after washing away the old medium and non-internalized h/CDs@CPT. After 2 h of incubation, the viability of the cells was analyzed using a microplate reader (Bio-Rad Model 680 XR, USA) at 450 nm. Live and dead cell analyses were performed by comparison with a control experiment.

The HeLa and 4 T1 cancer cells were cultured overnight in a specified amount of biological medium using a 96-well glass plate at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. The cells were then treated with a new medium containing concentration gradients of the h/CDs and incubated overnight. Then, the cells were rinsed twice with PBS and supplied with fresh medium before irradiating with a 532 nm laser source (1.12 W/cm2) for PDT treatment evaluation. The cells were washed with PBS to remove dead cells upon ROS generation via the PDT effect, and fresh medium containing 10 µL of WST-1 was added to the wells. A microplate reader was used to measure the optical absorbance at 450 nm, and a live cell count was performed with reference to the control experiment.

The in vitro ROS-responsive drug-releasing properties of the h/CDs@CPT NVs were studied under 532 nm laser irradiation. First, the cells were cultured using 96-well glass plate as explained in section 2.7.1, then different concentrations of the h/CDs@CPT NVs were injected into the cells along with fresh medium. After overnight incubation, the cells were washed with PBS to eliminate non-internalized h/CDs@CPT. Subsequently, a fixed amount of new medium was added to the cells before illumination with a 532 nm laser source (1.12 W/cm2) for 10 min. As with previously described experiments, the WST-1 solution was used to examine live and dead cells using a microplate reader at 450 nm. The outcomes were compared to those of a control experiment in which no h/CDs@CPT NVs were injected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro fluorescence imaging and quantitative ROS detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the fluorescence imaging capabilities of the h/CDs@CPT NVs, HeLa and 4 T1 cells were seeded overnight in six-well glass plates at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. The cells were fed varying concentrations of the h/CDs@CPT NVs dispersed in fresh medium and left for 8 h. The cells were then rinsed with PBS to remove any non-ingested h/CDs@CPT or contaminants. The cells were initially treated with 2 mL of ethanol (70 %) before being incubated with 2 mL of DAPI (staining agent) for 20 min according to the manufacturer’s protocol. The cells were then rinsed with PBS and mounted on glass slides. Cellular photographs were obtained using confocal microscopy (Leica TCS SP2 with a Leica 63x oil immersion objective NA = 1.4), with excitation wavelengths set at 406 and 561 nm for blue and red cellular images, respectively.

DCFH-DA probe molecule was used to detect intracellular ROS. The HeLa and 4 T1 cells were incubated with varying amounts of the h/CDs@CPT NVs for 8 h before absorbing 10 µL of DCFH-DA. The well plate was then subjected to 532 nm laser irradiation for 10 min. Next, the cells were washed three times with PBS, and fluorescence microscopy was used to acquire blue, green, and red cellular images at 405, 488, and 561 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively.

The in vitro cell-killing efficiency of the h/CDs@CPT NVs was further validated by a quantitative investigation. In this experiment, we used a trypan blue staining reagent to count the number of living and dead cells. HeLa cells were initially grown overnight on a 24-well plate. These cells were provided with various doses of the h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs and then incubated for 8 h at 37 ˚C. The cells were then washed with PBS to remove any non-injected h/CDs@CPT before being treated with a 532 nm laser source to assess the PDT and chemotherapeutic effects of the h/CDs@CPT NVs. Next, 100 µL of trypan blue solution was added to the well plate to stain the membranes of the dead cells. The numbers of live and dead cells were compared to those in the control experiment, which was performed using either 532 nm laser irradiation or h/CDs@CPT NVs.

The optical performance of the as-prepared N/CDs, NCl/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs were evaluated using standard QYs measurements. Rhodamine B, a red-emissive fluorescent dye (Φ = 68.3 % in anhydrous ethanol), was used as a reference solution to compute the various QYs. The optical yields of the as-synthesized CDs were calculated using the following equation:

where Φs and Φr represent the QYs of the CDs and rhodamine B dye, respectively; the area of the PL peak for the CDs and the reference dye was abbreviated as As and Ar, respectively. as and ar were used to explain the absorbance of the CDs and reference dye recorded at the excitation wavelength (0.050–0.059), respectively; and the refractive index values of the CDs and rhodamine B are denoted as ns and nr, respectively.

Surface characterization of the as-prepared product was performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) Model: ESCALAB 250 VG Scientific ESCALAB 250: Manufacturer: VG Scientific and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) Model: Tecnai F20 G2 FETEM Manufacturer: Philips Model: JEM-2100F Manufacturer: JEOL. The chemical bonding interactions of the surface atoms were examined using proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR), and 1H NMR spectra of the specimens were recorded at room temperature on a Bruker Advance III HD-600 spectrometer operating at 400 MHz using d-chloroform (CDCl3) as a solvent. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted using an FTS-3500 spectrometer at 25 °C. Surface charge properties was assessed by Zetasizer 2000 (MALVERN). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Model: Nano-ZS90; Manufacturer: MALVERN) was applied to record the hydrodynamic size distribution of h/CDs@CPT NVs. The photophysical properties of the different types of heteroatom-doped CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs were determined via UV–PL spectroscopy using a Duetta photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Scientific). The PL lifetime was studied using a time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) (PicoQuant NanoHarp 250) Model from: NanoHarp Manufacturer: Pico Quantity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical characterization of heteroatom doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Heteroatom-doped red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using a one-pot microwave pyrolysis technique, as illustrated in Scheme S1. Optical studies of these various CDs were conducted in aqueous solution using optical spectroscopy. The absorption spectra of undoped N/CDs (Fig. 1 a) exhibit typical characteristic bands at 240 nm denoting sp2 hybridized carbon, 300 nm denoting n → π* transitions of the extended aromatic structure, and a wide absorption at 502 nm (magnified picture, Fig. 1a) related to the low-energy state group and the π → π* transition of the electronic structure, which is consistent with recent reports [50,51]. The absorption spectra reveal no significant differences after surface passivation with (perchloric acid) PA, indicating that the surface passivating agent does not alter the CDs core-structure. However, the PL spectroscopic analysis reveals that after doping the N/CDs with increasing volumes of PA solution, the PL intensity gradually increases until the dopant dosage exceeds 0.6 mmol, as shown in Fig. 1b. As shown in Table S1, the relative quantum yield (QYs) of the as-prepared NCl/CDs are determined using rhodamine B as a reference. The QYs of our most pristine NCl/CDs (0.6 mmol PA dopant) reach up to 16.59 %, compared to undoped N/CDs (0.66 %). The QYs of the NCl/CDs progressively increase along with the dopant ratio and then start to fall with excess PA (0.8 mmol). In contrast, the PL peak of the NCl/CDs swiftly moved to a shorter wavelength (598 nm) than that of N/CDs, initially centered at 628 nm. Owing to their equivalent ionic radii, the introduction of the Cl dopant ions enables the replacement of the carbon center by the guest ion (Cl-), resulting in a widened band gap and shift in the emission center. Furthermore, the photo-optical characteristics of the as-prepared NCl/CDs are modified by employing l-cysteine (LC) and l-selenocysteine (LS) passivating agents. The UV absorption spectra of the NClS/CDs were collected, as shown in Fig. 1c, confirming that the NClS/CDs mimic the absorption spectra of the NCl/CDs. The spectra show three different bands at 240 nm, 299 nm, and near 500 nm (inset picture, Fig. 1c), which are related to the sp2 carbonic network, n → π transitions, and π → π* transitions, respectively. Likewise, the PL spectra in Fig. 1d demonstrate that after incorporating LC (S/N dopant source), the emission intensity of the NClS/CDs is significantly raised with increasing dopant concentration up to 0.02 mmol, and then begins to quench with a further increase in the doping ratio. Notably, the NClS/CDs exhibit an outstanding QYs of 30.70 % with 0.01 mmol of added LC, almost twofold that of the NCl/CDs (16.59 %). However, when the LC ratio is adjusted to 0.02, 0.04, and 0.06 mmol, the QYs of the NClS/CDs decrease to 25.42 %, 15.64 %, and 7.76 %, respectively (see Table S2). In addition, we devised a dual-doping strategy for the NCl/CDs utilizing the LS dopants, culminating in tri-doped NClSe/CDs with a higher emission intensity than that of the NCl/CDs and comparable absorption band (Fig. 1e) with spectral features analogous to those of NCl/CDs, except for the disappearance of the band around 500 nm. The PL peak of the NClSe/CDs appear at 598 nm, as with the NCl/CDs (Fig. 1f); however, the optical QYs of the as-prepared NClSe/CDs substantially increases to 42.89 % for 0.02 mmol of LS, and then decreases to 37.23 and 34.67 % (Table S3) with 0.04 and 0.06 mmol of LS, respectively. To investigate the effective integrations and chemical states of the Se, S, and Cl dopant ions in the CDs structure further, we conducted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies (Fig. 2 a). The survey spectra of the N/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs reveal distinctive peaks of C (1 s) at 292 eV, N (1 s) at 401 eV, and O (1 s) at 524 eV, suggesting the presence of compositional constituents. Remarkably, the signal maxima at 198, 170, and 69 eV correspond to the Cl (2p), S (2p), and Se (3d) configurations, respectively, in the NClS/CDs and NClSe/CDs, suggesting the flawless integration of the dopant ions into the CDs structure. More importantly, high-resolution XPS (HR–XPS) results of the NClS/CDs show two integrated peaks at 166 and 162.7 eV associated with 2p2/3 and 2p1/3 coupling, respectively, for the S 2p spectra (Fig. 2b). In addition, the Cl 2p spectra (Fig. 2c) are deconvoluted into two signals observed at 209 and 211.9 eV related to the ClO3 and ClO4 bonding species. The C 1 s spectra split into three signal peaks at 284.6, 286.8, and 289.1 eV, which are ascribed to the CC/CN, CN/CC, and CO/CS bonding (Fig. 2d). The maximal intensity of the CO/CS interaction suggests that the surface of the NClS/CDs contains a large number of sulfide and hydroxyl functional groups. The characteristic peak of N 1 s is deconvoluted into four partitions (Fig. 2e) at 389, 400.2, 402, and 403.9 eV for CN, CN/NS, C-(N)3, and NO chemical bonding, respectively. Fig. 2f depicts the deconvoluted spectra of O 1 s at 531.6 and 534.4 eV related to the CO and CO connections, respectively. The chemical bonding of CC/CC is prominent in the undoped CDs; however, following PA and LC doping, most of the sp2 hybridized structure in the NClS/CDs is oxidized, forming not only a CO/CS interface but also CN and CO interactions (Table S4). The XPS analysis of NCl/CDs was discussed in the supporting information (Figure S2). Besides, the HR-XPS of NClSe/CDs (Fig. 3 a) produces triplet peaks at 284.5, 286.8, and 288.4 eV for C 1 s ascribed to the CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO bonding type, respectively. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 3b, the N 1 s spectra is integrated into four peaks located at 398, 399.8, 402.2, and 404.6 eV for CN, CN, N-(C)3, and NO interaction, respectively. Two deconvoluted spectra of O 1 s are identified at 531.3 and 533.5 eV related to CO and CO bonding, respectively (Fig. 3c). Furthermore, XPS analyses of guest ions such as Cl and Se are displayed in Fig. 3d and 3e, respectively. The signal peaks observed at 209 and 212 eV represent ClO3 and ClO4 chemical interactions, respectively (Fig. 3d). As shown in Fig. 3e, the Se 3d peak is deconvoluted into two signals shown at 52.7 and 55.8 eV associated with the 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 hybridized orbitals of the Se ions. Additionally, the chemical interactions of the surface atoms in the N/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs were studied using FTIR spectroscopy, as depicted in Fig. S3a. The interpretation of FTIR and Raman spectra was provided in the supporting information.

The enhanced optical properties of the NClS/CDs and NClSe/CDs were further validated using the time-resolved PL (TR/PL) technique. As shown in Fig. 3f, the PL decay curves of the N/CDs, NCl/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs are drawn using a biexponential function (

), and the detailed parameters, including the average lifetime, are summarized in Table S5, where the average lifetime (τav) was obtained using τav =

. In this context, A and τ represent the relative amplitudes and lifetime information, respectively. According to graphical analysis and lifetime information, the N/CDs have an average lifetime of 1.11 ns, whereas after doping with Cl, the lifetime slightly improves to 3.34 ns, implying an enhanced optical performance of the NCl/CDs solution. The NClS/CDs demonstrate 5.31 ns for τ1 (A = 25.6 %) and 10.7 ns for τ2 (36.4 %), with an average lifetime calculated to be 6.9 ns. Regarding NClSe/CDs, τ1 and τ2 are 9.3 and 10.28 ns, respectively. The PL lifetime increases in the order: N/CDs, NCl/CDs, NClS/CDs, and NClSe/CDs, which is linearly related to the quantum efficiency of the solution. The integration of heteroatoms S, N, Se, and Cl increases the radiative transition channel of the sp2 carbon network. This improves the transition of the electrons at distinct energy levels. As shown in Fig. S3c, when the doping phenomenon is augmented, the electron density state (which causes lower optical quality) diminishes, resulting in better emission properties. Other dominant factors, such as crosslinked enhanced emission (CEE), play an important role in achieving the highest quantum efficiencies. Covalently and non-covalently bonded heteroatoms in the CDs structure restrict non-radiative recombination pathways via the CEE mechanism, resulting in a longer PL lifetime. Briefly, higher-energy electrons emit PL either by reverting to the ground state or by phosphorescence. Not all stimulated electrons induce emission qualities because of the existence of non-radiative channels. The incorporation of heteroatoms (N, Cl, S, and Se) block a non-radiative phenomenon by reducing the vibrational movements of the excited electrons, thereby improving the optical properties. Following doping, newly generated coupling/overlapping of atomic orbitals occurs, producing variations in the bond length between the branching functional groups, resulting in a rearrangement of the energy levels, and prompting electrons to adopt a radiative route. Based on these findings, the NClS/CDs exhibit exceptional QYs and long-range absorption capacities, which offer significant promise for phototherapeutic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and therapeutic studies of h/CDs@CPT prodrug NVs
ce:section.ce:para: The h/CDs@CPT prodrug NVs were prepared at room temperature, as shown in Scheme 1 , with essential components including the h/CDs and TLCPT integrated together via EDC/NHS-sulfo crosslinkers. Initially, anhydrous 3-MPA and acetone were treated in a N2-gas environment with saturated dry HCl to produce an ROS-cleavable TL branch. The hydroxyl group of TL was then substituted via crosslinkers, such as TEA and TCBC, before being coupled with CPT molecules through a DMAP crosslinking agent, resulting in a TLCPT prodrug. The TLCPT molecule was then conjugated by activating the amine terminal group of the h/CDs nanocarrier using EDC/NHS-sulfo crosslinkers, and the resultant solution (h/CDs@CPT) was purified by centrifugation to eliminate unloaded drug molecules and DMSO residue. TEM and FTIR spectroscopy were used to characterize h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs. TEM images of h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs with monodispersed spherical nanodot morphologies are displayed in Fig. 4 a-4c, respectively. The d-spacing information was found to be 0.33 nm, as shown in Fig. 4b, which is consistent with graphitic carbon structure. The DLS measurement of h/CDs@CPT as shown in Fig. 4c, demonstrating that h/CDs@CPT owned 8.61 nm. Furthermore, the polydispersity index (PDI) of the h/CDs@CPT was also determined to be 0.40. Fig. S4a and S4b present the 1H NMR spectral data for TL and TLCPT, respectively, with a peak for the thiol linkage at δ = 1.56, confirming the presence of ROS-sensitive TL in the prodrug molecule. The 1H NMR data from TL (Fig. S4a) reveals the presence of protonated hydroxyl ions and an alkyl group as distinct signal peaks identified in the figure as a-d. Notably, the TLCPT spectra (Fig. S4b) produces signals corresponding to unsaturated benzyl protons in addition to the TL signal, indicating that the CPT drug molecule is conjoined with TL through a ketonic functional (COC) group. We also demonstrate the breakdown of TLCPT in response to ROS by introducing H2O2 and Fe3+ ions to the TLCPT solution at room temperature. Fig. S4c illustrates the absence of the TL characteristic signal at δ = 1.56 ppm, demonstrating that the radical species degrades the TL, which is consistent with the results of previous studies [44,47]. Although a minor concentration of TL persists in CPT after ROS cleavage, it has little influence on drug efficacy and may be degraded and undergo structural inactivation in lysosomes. Furthermore, the loading of the TLCPT prodrug onto the surface of the h/CDs was verified by MS analysis. The mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of h/CDs@CPT (Fig. S4d) at 242.3, 348.52, and 582.43 reveals the presence of TL, CPT, and TLCPT fragments, respectively. These 1H NMR and MS analyses provide conclusive evidence that h/CDs@CPT NVs are successfully produced. Fig. S5 a shows the FTIR spectra of TL, which has a wide absorption band at 3230 cm−1 attributed to the –OH interaction, and peaks at 2970, 1710, and 670 cm−1 assigned to the CH, CO stretching, and CS bending of the TL molecule, respectively. The sharp peak at 1150 cm−1 is attributed to COC bonding, which binds the TL and CPT components together. In addition, the stretching frequencies of –OH, CH, CO, aromatic H, and CS bending are identified at 3430, 2970, 1710, 1650, and 650 cm−1, respectively, validating TLCPT formation. The FTIR spectra of the h/CDs@CPT NVs produces characteristic signals at 3430, 1700, and 1210 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching frequencies of –OH/–NH2, CO, and CO bending, respectively. The appearance of SH stretching and C-Cl bending at 2560 and 520 cm−1, respectively, corroborates the integration of the h/CDs and the occurrence of the COC and CS linkages at 1120 and 650 cm−1, respectively, thereby confirming the existence of a TLCPT-enriched h/CDs surface. The zeta potential values of h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs reveals a positive surface charge (Fig. S5b), approximately 16.8 and 3.4 mV, respectively. The h/CDs exhibit highly positive characteristics related to the presence of a –NH2 rich surface, while the S2- and –OH ions of TLCPT cause neutralization of the h/CDs@CPT positive surface. These findings suggest that the CPT prodrug is efficiently loaded onto the h/CDs nanocarrier, leading to the formation of the h/CDs@CPT NVs. The photographic images shown in Figure S6 demonstrated that h/CDs@CPT NVs exhibited better aqueous dispersibility for up to five days and then started to form agglomeration at the bottom of the sample bottle. Similarly, the images showed good dispersibility in other biological mediums such as MEME and RPMI. The optical spectra of the h/CDs@CPT solution are presented in Fig. S7a, exhibiting pronounced shoulder peaks at 360 nm and 398 nm for the TLCPT molecule and broad absorption at 600 nm for the h/CDs. Additionally, the absorbance spectra of the h/CDs@CPT solution demonstrate intense shoulder peaks near 360 nm, 398 nm, and 600 nm, suggesting effective CPT integration onto the h/CDs nanocarrier. The emission spectra for the h/CDs@CPT colloidal solution (Fig. S7b), on the other hand, demonstrate a prominent emission peak at approximately 600 nm owing to the presence of the h/CDs, validating the assembly of h/CDs@CPT prodrug NVs. The overall optical characterization of h/CDs@CPT reveals that the TLCPT prodrug is entirely assimilated into the surface of the h/CDs, and it does not impair the fluorescence capabilities of the h/CDs.

Considering the remarkable light absorption features of the h/CDs in the visible range, we demonstrate the ROS-generating performance of the h/CDs@CPT NVs upon 532 nm laser irradiation. In this study, a yellowish 1,4-diphenyl-2,3-benzofuran (DPBF) solution was used as a ROS-responsive molecule, and following contact with ROS, the DPBF structure undergoes a rearrangement mechanism (refer to Figure S8a) to produce colorless 1,3-dibenzoylbenezen (DBB), resulting in the quenching of the absorption spectra at 416 nm. The absorption spectra of DPBF alone remains unchanged upon laser irradiation for 30 min (Figure S9a); however, after the introduction of the h/CDs solution, the absorption spectra of DPBF rapidly quenches with increasing irradiation time, as shown in Figure S9b, confirming that h/CDs possess ROS generation properties in combination with lasers for PDT of cancer. Similarly, drug-loaded h/CDs (h/CDs@CPT) display a good ROS-generating performance when subjected to laser irradiation, giving time- and laser-dependent degradation of DPBF absorption spectra (Fig. 5a), suggesting its potential for use as a PS agent. Figure S9c summarizes the DPBF degradation phenomena in the presence of the h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT solutions. Approximately 99 % of the DPBF concentration deteriorates after 10 min of incubation when compared to the control experiment, corroborating our previous findings. The h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT, in contrast to other PS agents, completely deactivate the DPBF molecule to DBB in a short period of time (10 min), suggesting PS as an excellent alternative in nanomedicine. Furthermore, the formation and types of ROS were identified using EPR spectroscopy and ROS-probe molecules. TEMP is oxidized to nitrosyl intermediates after interacting with the 1O2 radical, generating a set of characteristic peaks (Figure S8b). In this experiment, singlet oxygen (1O2) radical species were monitored in the presence and absence of laser irradiation by applying a 100 mM TEMP radical trapper. In the absence of either laser or h/CDs, no EPR signal is observed (Figure S9d); nevertheless, triplet signal peaks are produced for TEMP-oxo-4‑nitrosyl when h/CDs are treated with the 532 nm laser, confirming the generation of 1O2. Similarly, Fig. 5b demonstrates that in the presence of the h/CDs@CPT NVs and laser irradiation, intense triplet characteristic peaks are obtained, confirming the formation of 1O2 radicals. However, no signal peak is identified for TEMP, where one of the substrates, either h/CDs@CPT or laser source, is absent. The intensities of the peaks in Figures S9d and 5b increase with increasing concentrations of the h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT, respectively. Upon light illumination, the h/CDs undergo absorption of photo energy and the electrons are excited to a higher energy state in the singlet state (Figure S9e). The singlet-state electrons are transformed to a triplet state through an ISC mechanism to accelerate the breakdown of molecular oxygen into harmful radical species. It has been reported that heteroatom dopants effectively accelerate the transformation of the singlet state to the triplet state via a heavy-atom effect mechanism. As a result, the S and Cl dopant ions in the h/CDs structure facilitate ISC to enhance the formation of triplet states, thereby enhancing ROS generation. Combining these findings, the h/CDs@CPT NVs efficiently produce cytotoxic radical species such as 1O2, suggesting their potential candidacy for PDT for cancer.

The mechanism of drug dissociation and transportation from the carrier and the surrounding environment determine the drug-release phenomena. We establish a ROS-responsive TL system in conjunction with laser irradiation. After exposing the h/CDs@CPT NVs to a 532 nm laser source, a large amount of ROS is produced, prompting the abrupt collapse of thiol bonding and the release of the ROS-cleaved CPT drug (as shown in Scheme 1). Accordingly, the release of the drug is demonstrated under laser irradiation, where the h/CDs@CPT NVs solution is first treated for 10 min with a 532 nm laser source, and then dialyzed against a 0.1 M buffer solution (pH = 5). The absorbance at 368 nm is measured at every stage, and the percentage release is calculated. Figure S10a shows the calibration curve obtained for free CPT molecules at different concentrations. The release of CPT is more favorable under acidic conditions (Figure S10b), with 1.3 %, 4.2 %, and 11.7 % of CPT released after 48 h at pH 5.5, 6, and 7.4, respectively, confirming that the pH-responsive drug delivery system is suitable for the tumor microenvironment. Upon laser exposure, the CPT release is 50.2 %, which is expectedly higher than that of under dark conditions (11.7 %), and drops to 25.2 % in the presence of a H2O2 solution, confirming the ROS-triggering properties of CPT (Fig. 5c). It is noted that the CPT released in the first 8 h significantly increases to 39 %, and becomes saturated for the remaining response time, presumably owing to the short lifetime of the ROS. Thus, the CPT release does not exceed 100 %. This may be due to the hydrophobicity of the drug molecule, which restricts the quantity of CPT in the release medium. This is supported by the presence of CPT precipitate/aggregates observed at the bottom of the dialysis bags. ROS-induced CPT release is further confirmed using exogenous ROS supply, where a mixture of H2O2 (100 mM) and Fe2+ (10 mM) solutions was added to the system as a control group. Using Fenton-like activity, Fe2+ ions facilitate the catalytic decomposition reaction of H2O2 and produce very reactive hydroxyl (•OH) radicals, which can also efficiently cleave the TL bond. After 48 h, approximately 55 % of the CPT is released, which is close to the results achieved in the presence of the laser. The synergistic effect of the laser and Fe2+/H2O2 was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 5e, with 71.6 % CPT release obtained after 48 h. This indicates that intrinsic and extrinsic ROS sources induce remarkable CPT-drug release from h/CDs@CPT NVs (Fig. 5d). To obtain more information about the drug releasing parameters, we conducted a kinetics studies using Korsmeyer-Peppas model. The constant was determined using the equation at n = M(t)/M∞, where a and n are fitting parameters and M(t)/M∞ signifies the amount of drug released at time t. The regression value (Table S6) obtained from zero order is significantly smaller than that obtained from first order, suggesting that the CPT-releasing phenomenon was identical to the first order model. At the same time, the n value can be used to predict the potential mechanism of drug release. The combination of Laser/Fe3+/H2O2 achieves the highest n-value, suggesting that the cumulative effect of laser and Fenton-like reaction generates a huge amount of ROS capable of destroying the TL bridge and releasing the drug. These findings demonstrate that our prodrug system is an ROS-sensitive, on-demand, and regulated drug delivery system, all of which are beneficial for dissipating negative health effects and enhancing safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and therapeutic evaluations of h/CDs@CPT prodrug in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
The possibility of using this h/CDs@CPT prodrug in nanomedicine applications, as well as its therapeutic effectiveness were further validated through an in vitro assay. The well-known WST-1 reagent was used to determine live cell counts. After interacting with intracellular enzyme, the WST-1 molecules are reduced (Figure S11), resulting in a dark-yellow formazan structure that is identified at 450 nm using a microplate reader. The concentration gradients of the h/CDs solutions were incubated with skin cancer cells (HeLa), and breast cancer cells (4 T1) for different time intervals (24 h and 48 h) to confirm the cytotoxicity of the h/CDs@CPT NVs, resulting in excellent biocompatibility toward HeLa and 4 T1 cancer cells up to a concentration of 200 ppm h/CDs. The fact that the cell viability starts to drop, reaching 83.2 % and 87.3 % on the second day of incubation for the HeLa and 4 T1 cells, respectively, demonstrates that h/CDs could be a safer product for application in cancer treatment in the absence of laser treatment (Fig. 6
a and 6b). Next, the in vitro therapeutic efficacy of the h/CDs was evaluated in the presence of 532 nm laser irradiation. Before being exposed to laser irradiation, the h/CDs were allowed to co-culture with the HeLa and 4 T1 cells, as explained in the experimental section. Fig. 6c and 6d show that the number of living cells gradually decreases as the concentration of the h/CDs increases. Laser combination triggers the formation of ROS, thereby causing cancer cell death owing to the PDT effect. Remarkably, the cell viability drops to 54.2 % and 27.3 % in the presence of 200 ppm-h/CDs and laser exposure for the HeLa and 4 T1 cells, respectively. However, in the absence of the h/CDs, the live cell counts remain the same when irradiated with a 532 nm laser.

After treated by a 532 nm laser source, h/CDs generate cytotoxic ROS inside the mitochondria of the cell membrane, resulting in cellular damage or malfunction of the DNA structure of the cells and leading to cell death. Following the demonstration of the capacity of the h/CDs to generate ROS in cancer cells, the ROS-triggered drug release of the h/CDs@CPT NVs was demonstrated using in vitro cancer cells. The h/CDs@CPT NVs-incubated cancer cells were treated with 532 nm laser irradiation, and CPT-induced cancer cell death was verified accordingly. Fig. 6c and 6d show that the viable cell count diminishes with increasing concentration of the h/CDs@CPT and irradiation time. As the concentration of h/CDs@CPT increases to 200 ppm followed by laser treatment, the number of live cells decreases to 39.3 % and 24.1 % for the HeLa and 4 T1 cells, respectively, as compared with the non-irradiated h/CDs@CPT-incubated cancer cells, which reach only 94.1 % and 93.8 %, respectively. This suggests that the release of CPT in vitro is induced under laser irradiation and acidic conditions. Upon 532 nm laser irradiation, h/CDs@CPT produces ROS in the mitochondria, which triggers the breakdown of the TK linkage, and releases CPT-drug, reaching the nucleus via diffusion and causing DNA inactivation, and finally leading to massive damage to the cells (Fig. 5d). Moreover, the half-maximum inhibition rate (IC50) of the as-obtained h/CDs@CPT is estimated from the graph, where h/CDs@CPT exhibits IC50 values of 153.32 and 126.8 ppm for the HeLa and 4 T1 cells respectively, exemplifying that nearly 50 % of cell death occurs at the aforementioned concentration of the h/CDs@CPT NVs.

The fluorescent properties of the h/CDs@CPT NVs enable their exploitation not only as phototherapeutic molecules but also as potential biomarker molecules in cancer cell detection. The HeLa and 4 T1 cells were incubated with varying concentrations of h/CDs@CPT NVs overnight, and a fluorescent dye (DAPI) was used as a nucleus-staining agent. Fig. 6e and S12 show bright-red cellular pictures of the HeLa and 4 T1 cells in the presence of the h/CDs@CPT NVs. The luminescence power of the red-emissive images increases with increasing h/CDs@CPT concentrations, resulting in highly fluorescent cellular images as illustrated in Fig. 6e, row II. Unlike bulky biomaterials that are typically uptaken by cells through phagocytosis, the h/CDs@CPT undergo diverse endocytosis mechanisms in which the surface functional groups of the CDs interface with the clathrin and caveolin protein components of the cell membranes and effectively penetrate the cellular structure. Likewise, 4 T1 cells readily absorb the h/CDs@CPT NVs and produce red fluorescence, with the fluorescence increasing as the h/CDs@CPT concentration. In this study, a nucleic fixing reagent (DAPI) was used, which displays blue fluorescence images at 406 excitation wavelengths (row II of Fig. 6e and column II of Figure S12). The overlaying picture of the HeLa and 4 T1 cells (row III of Fig. 6e and S12) demonstrates the accumulation of DAPI in the nucleus center and h/CDs@CPT surrounding the nucleus organelles. To confirm the ROS generation performance of the h/CDs@CPT NVs at the cellular level, the ROS-responsive probe molecule, 2′-7′dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) was utilized according to manufacturer protocol. In the cell membranes, the uptake of non-fluorescent DCFH-DA is first deacetylated by cellular esterases to a non-fluorescent 2′-7′dichlorofluorescin (DCFH), which is then oxidized by ROS into a green-emitting DCF• molecule, as illustrated in Fig. 7a. Based on this principle, various concentrations of the h/CDs@CPT NVs are incubated with the HeLa and 4 T1 cells before treatment with a 532 nm laser. Fig. 7b shows that bright green cellular photographs are obtained owing to the formation of the DCF• molecules using the HeLa cells, confirming the generation of ROS upon combining h/CDs@CPT and laser excitation. However, in the absence of h/CDs@CPT, no green fluorescence is observed, as shown in the image. Similarly, the 4 T1 cells show effective intracellular ROS generation (Figure S13), with green luminescent images attributed to the formation of the DCF• molecules after combination with ROS produced in the mitochondria of the cells. The green luminescent image became more robust with increasing h/CDs@CPT concentration, indicating concentration-dependent ROS generation by the h/CDs@CPT NVs. Experimental evidence and in vitro therapeutic evaluations verify that the h/CDs@CPT NVs have the potential for use in cancer detection and treatment. The mean fluorescence intensity of the h/CDs@CPT and DCFH channel images progressively intensifies with increasing h/CDs@CPT concentration (Figures S14a and S14b), confirming the dose-dependence of the ROS-generating ability and imaging properties of the h/CDs@CPT NVs.

Trypan blue staining was used to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of the h/CDs@CPT NVs systematically, as shown in Figure S15. After incubating HeLa cells with various doses of the h/CDs@CPT NVs solution, the wells were irradiated with a 532 nm laser to examine PDT and PDT-driven chemotherapy, while dead cells were identified with trypan blue reagent. The cell-only photographs, in the presence of laser treatment, display a clear cellular morphology with no cell death, confirming that the laser alone does not promote cell clearance. Similarly, incubating cells with the h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs in the absence of laser irradiation does not result in tumor cell death, implying that our product owned a better biocompatibility. However, after laser exposure, a considerable number of blue spots is observed, as shown in the image, demonstrating that the combination of the laser and h/CDs@CPT triggered accelerated cell death owing to ROS production and PDT-driven chemotherapy. To investigate the PDT-alone effects of our product, we utilized the h/CDs instead of the h/CDs@CPT NVs. The ensuing microscopic image displays a substantial number of blue-stained cellular morphologies owing to the efficient production of ROS upon laser irradiation, indicating that h/CDs have the potential to be applied as a PDT agent. Compared to the h/CDs, the h/CDs@CPT NVs reveal a greater antitumor efficacy, which is attributed to the beneficial effects of PDT and PDT-driven chemotherapy. Cell death is exacerbated under both conditions (h/CDs and h/CDs@CPT NVs), as the proportion of the blue spots produced increases in comparison to the non-blue spots. The effective production of ROS by the h/CDs@CPT NVs in the cells' mitochondria not only causes binding to the DNA strand and DNA malfunction, but also prompts thiol linkage breakdown and the release of the CPT-drug, which subsequently binds to and denatures the DNA in the nucleus, thereby accelerating cancer cell death. The number of cell death was determined by counting the blue microscopic images as shown in Figure S16, which demonstrate an exponential growth with increasing concentration, supporting our evidence for increased cell death. Compared with the h/CDs, the h/CDs@CPT NVs produce a stronger intensity, implying better killing efficacy owing to the synergistic effects of PDT and chemotherapy.

In this work, for the first time, we successfully designed and produced ROS-responsive novel prodrug nanovesicles for synergistic local-chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy of cancer using a CDs as both a ROS-generator and nanocarrier. High-performance heteroatom-doped CDs (NClS/CDs and NClSe/CDs) with exceptional optical response were first synthesized. In comparison to the undoped N/CDs, the NClS/CDs and NClSe/CDs not only exhibited superior photophysical performances with higher QYs of 30.7 % and 42.86 %, respectively, but also possessed light-activated ROS-generating properties and photochemical activity. Under laser irradiation, the h/CDs@CPT NVs demonstrated efficient ROS generation capacity for PDT of cancer. The as-prepared product, h/CDs@CPT NVs, exhibited considerable drug release ability (50.2 %) under acidic conditions and laser combination. Compared to the physically-loaded drug delivery system, our chemically connected chemotherapeutic platform could minimize the untimely drug migration and transportation to the malignant area synchronously. In vitro cellular studies confirmed that the h/CDs@CPT NVs exhibited low toxicity toward HeLa and 4 T1 cancer cells in the absence of a laser source, whereas the cell viabilities were dramatically reduced upon laser combination because of the synergistic effects of PDT and chemotherapy. Furthermore, cellular imaging analysis showed ultra-fluorescent red-emissive photographs in an attempt to identify potential biomarkers for cancer detection. Overall, the enhanced water solubility of the h/CDs@CPT NVs and the high therapeutic efficacy via PDT and chemotherapy mechanisms could be an alternative strategy to eradicate cancer cells.

Rasal: Formal analysis. Worku Batu Dirersa: Formal analysis. Chih-Ching Huang: Formal analysis. Neralla Vijayakameswara Rao: Formal analysis. Je-Hsin Chen: Formal analysis. Jia-Yaw Chang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
